questions.text: The fractional change in resistance per kelvin is known as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect

The temperature coefficient of resistance is a measure of how much the electrical resistance of a material changes with temperature. It is expressed in units of ohms per ohm per degree Celsius (Ω/Ω/°C) or ohms per ohm per kelvin (Ω/Ω/K). This coefficient is denoted by the symbol α (alpha) and is an important factor in understanding and designing electronic circuits, particularly when dealing with components like resistors whose resistance can vary with temperature.
The statement "The fractional change in resistance per kelvin is known as Thermal coefficient" is not entirely accurate. The correct term is the "temperature coefficient of resistance."

the "volumetric coefficient of expansion" typically refers to a measure of how the volume of a material changes with temperature. It is not directly related to the change in electrical resistance with temperature.

The "linear coefficient of expansion" typically relates to the change in length (dimension) of a material with temperature. It is a measure of how much a material expands or contracts linearly with changes in temperature. This concept is not directly related to changes in electrical resistance.

questions.explanation: The fractional change in resistance per kelvin is called the temperature coefficient.The temperature coefficient of resistance is a measure of how much the electrical resistance of a material changes with temperature. It is expressed in units of ohms per ohm per degree Celsius (Ω/Ω/°C) or ohms per ohm per kelvin (Ω/Ω/K). This coefficient is denoted by the symbol α (alpha) and is an important factor in understanding and designing electronic circuits, particularly when dealing with components like resistors whose resistance can vary with temperatureResistance coefficient, abbreviated as K, a dimensionless number, is how much resistance to the flow an obstacle has. Volumetric coefficient increases in volume per unit original volume per Kelvin rise in temperature is called the coefficient of volumetric expansion.The ratio increase in length original length for the 1-degree rise in temperature is called the coefficient of linear expansion

questions.text: The deviation of I-V graph from straight line is due to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Decrease in temperature and decrease in resistance
options.explanation: If temperature decreases and resistance also decreases, it is less likely to cause a significant deviation from a straight-line I-V graph. Typically, resistance tends to increase with decreasing temperature.

options.text: Increase in temperature and increase in resistance
options.explanation: When both temperature and resistance increase, this can lead to a more noticeable deviation from the straight-line behavior. Many materials exhibit an increase in resistance with temperature, and higher resistance can cause a steeper curve in the I-V graph.

options.text: Decrease in temperature and increase in resistance
options.explanation: This combination is common in many materials. As temperature decreases, the resistance often increases. The increase in resistance can contribute to a deviation from the straight-line behavior in the I-V graph.

options.text: Increase in temperature and decrease in resistance
options.explanation: This combination is less common for typical conductors. In most materials, as temperature increases, resistance also increases. However, certain materials like semiconductors may exhibit a decrease in resistance with increasing temperature under specific conditions.

questions.explanation: The most common scenario leading to a deviation from a straight-line I-V graph is an increase in temperature and an increase in resistance. This is often observed in conductors and is known as a positive temperature coefficient of resistance. As temperature rises, the resistance increases, causing a departure from the ideal linear behavior in the I-V graph.

questions.text: If you wish to decrease the resistance of a circuit, you will add the resistors in _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a series circuit, resistors are connected end-to-end, so the current flowing through each resistor is the same. When more resistors are added in series, the total resistance of the circuit increases. According to Ohm's Law (V = IR), if resistance increases and voltage remains constant, the current will decrease. Therefore, the statement contradicts the behavior of resistors in a series circuit.

In a parallel circuit, resistors are connected side by side, providing multiple paths for current to flow. When more resistors are added in parallel, the total resistance of the circuit decreases. This is because the combined paths offer less resistance than a single path. According to Ohm's Law, if resistance decreases and voltage remains constant, the current will increase. Therefore, this option correctly describes the behavior of resistors in a parallel circuit.

This option implies that adding more resistors, either in series or parallel, does not affect the equivalent resistance or total current of the circuit. However, this contradicts the fundamental principles of electrical circuits.

This option suggests that there is not enough information given to determine the effect of adding more resistors on the circuit. However, the behavior of resistors in series and parallel circuits is well-defined and can be determined based on the circuit configuration. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
This option appears to be unrelated to the question and does not provide any meaningful explanation in the context of resistors in circuits. Therefore, it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: As more and more resistors are added in parallel to a circuit, the equivalent resistance of the circuit decreases, and the total current of the circuit increases. Adding more resistors in parallel is equivalent to providing more branches through which charge can flow.

questions.text: If we increase the cross-section area of a conductor having a length L, the resistance R of that conductor will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If you increase the cross-sectional area (A) of a conductor while keeping its length (L) constant, the resistance (R) of that conductor will decrease as resistance is inversely propotional to area. it's evident that increasing the cross-sectional area (A) in the denominator will result in a decrease in resistance (R). This is why thicker conductors with larger cross-sectional areas generally have lower resistance compared to thinner conductors of the same material and length.

If you increase the cross-sectional area (A) of a conductor while keeping its length (L) constant, the resistance (R) of that conductor will decrease as resistance is inversely propotional to area. it's evident that increasing the cross-sectional area (A) in the denominator will result in a decrease in resistance (R). This is why thicker conductors with larger cross-sectional areas generally have lower resistance compared to thinner conductors of the same material and length.

If you increase the cross-sectional area (A) of a conductor while keeping its length (L) constant, the resistance (R) of that conductor will decrease as resistance is inversely propotional to area. it's evident that increasing the cross-sectional area (A) in the denominator will result in a decrease in resistance (R). This is why thicker conductors with larger cross-sectional areas generally have lower resistance compared to thinner conductors of the same material and length.

If you increase the cross-sectional area (A) of a conductor while keeping its length (L) constant, the resistance (R) of that conductor will decrease as resistance is inversely propotional to area. it's evident that increasing the cross-sectional area (A) in the denominator will result in a decrease in resistance (R). This is why thicker conductors with larger cross-sectional areas generally have lower resistance compared to thinner conductors of the same material and length.

questions.explanation: The resistance of a conductor decreases with increase in cross-sectional area. ○If we increase the area of cross section of conductor then More electrons results in more charge crossing the cross section, thus more current. Thus if you increase the area of cross section then resistance of the wire (keeping length and resistivity same) will decrease. Resistance R = ρ L/A Result: From the above discussion and the formula of resistance that with increase in area of cross section resistance decrease and vice versa.

questions.explanation: The SI unit of electrical resistivity is the ohm-meter (Ω⋅m). For example, if a 1 m × 1 m × 1 m solid cube of material has sheet contacts on two opposite faces, and the resistance between these contacts is 1 Ω, then the resistivity of the material is 1 Ω⋅m.

questions.text: One of the following is an Ohmic device:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Filament bulbs, especially incandescent bulbs, are not Ohmic devices. Their resistance increases with temperature, and their I-V (current-voltage) characteristics are nonlinear.

Semiconductor diodes are nonlinear devices and do not follow Ohm's Law. They exhibit a characteristic non-linear relationship between current and voltage, allowing current to flow more easily in one direction than the other.
Transistors are also nonlinear devices. The relationship between the collector current and collector-emitter voltage in a transistor is not a straight line, making transistors non-Ohmic

Copper wire, in its typical operating range, can be considered an Ohmic device. It usually follows Ohm's Law, and its resistance remains relatively constant over a wide range of voltages and currents. The I-V characteristics for a copper wire are generally linear.

questions.explanation: Copper wire is the Ohmic device in the given options. It exhibits Ohmic behavior within its normal operating conditions, meaning that its resistance remains constant and follows Ohm's Law over a range of voltages and currents.

questions.text: Terminal voltage Vi of the battery is greater than emf of the battery when:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a battery is charging, the external charger provides a higher voltage to overcome the battery's emf and its internal resistance. The formula becomes V = E + Ir, where V is the terminal voltage, E is the emf, I is the charging current, and r is the internal resistance. Therefore, V > E.

During discharging, the battery supplies current to an external circuit. The formula is V = E - Ir, indicating that the terminal voltage is less than the emf due to the voltage drop across the internal resistance.

When the battery is connected to a resistor R and is discharging, the terminal voltage is affected by the internal resistance. The formula V = E - Ir applies, which means the terminal voltage is less than the emf.
If a battery is connected to a voltmeter without any current flow, the voltmeter reads the emf, as the terminal voltage equals the emf (V = E) when there is no current flowing.

questions.explanation: When a battery is charging, it requires a higher external voltage to overcome its emf and internal resistance. This results in a terminal voltage (V) greater than the emf (E) of the battery, as described by the equation V = E + Ir. Option A is correct because it correctly identifies the condition under which the terminal voltage exceeds the emf. Options B, C, and D describe scenarios where the terminal voltage is either equal to or less than the emf due to the nature of discharging or when there is no current flow.

questions.text: The electrons in a conductor drift with a velocity of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor can vary depending on several factors, including the material of the conductor, the applied electric field, and the temperature. In general, the drift velocity is relatively small compared to the average thermal velocity of the electrons.In a typical metallic conductor, the drift velocity of electrons is on the order of millimeters per second (mm/s). For example, in a copper wire with a current of 1 ampere (A), the drift velocity of electrons is typically around 0.1 mm/s.It's important to note that the drift velocity is an average value and does not represent the instantaneous velocity of individual electrons. Individual electrons undergo random thermal motion and experience frequent collisions, which cause their velocity to fluctuate rapidly.The magnitude of the drift velocity can also be influenced by factors such as the cross-sectional area of the conductor, the number density of charge carriers (electrons), and the mobility of the charge carriers, which depends on the properties of the material.In summary, while the drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is relatively small, it plays a crucial role in determining the flow of electrical current through the conductor.The electrons in a conductor do not drift with a specific velocity. Instead, they exhibit a random motion known as electron diffusion. In a conducting material, such as a wire, electrons move in response to an electric field. When an electric field is applied across a conductor, the electrons experience a net force and move in the direction opposite to the field.However, it is important to note that the individual electron motion is highly random due to collisions with atoms and other electrons within the conductor. These collisions cause frequent changes in direction and speed, leading to an overall random motion of the electrons.Therefore, it is not accurate to describe the motion of electrons in a conductor as drifting with a specific velocity.
it is a fact
it is a fact
it is a fact

questions.explanation: Drift velocity refers to the average velocity at which charged particles, such as electrons, move through a conductor in the presence of an electric field. While individual electrons within a conductor undergo random motion, the application of an electric field causes a net directional movement of electrons. When an electric field is applied across a conductor, it exerts a force on the free electrons within the material. This force, known as the electric force, causes the electrons to accelerate in the direction opposite to the electric field. However, the electrons also experience frequent collisions with atoms or other electrons, which disrupt their motion and cause them to lose some of their momentum. The net effect of these collisions is that electrons experience a resistive force, known as the drag force, which opposes their motion. As a result, the electrons attain a steady-state average velocity known as the drift velocity. The drift velocity represents the average speed and direction at which electrons move through the conductor. It is important to note that the drift velocity is typically quite small. Even in a typical metallic conductor, the drift velocity of electrons is typically on the order of millimeters per second. This is due to the high number density of electrons and the large number of collisions they experience within the conductor. The concept of drift velocity helps in understanding phenomena such as electrical current, Ohm's Law, and the behavior of conductors under the influence of electric fields. Free electrons have a speed in the order of several hundred kilometers while the drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is 10-3 m/s. (PTB)

questions.text: A steady current of 5 A is drawn from an electric source at a voltage of 100 V. The power consumed (in Watts) is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the power consumed if the current is 0.05 A. However, the current is given as 5 A, so this option is incorrect.
This is the power consumed if the current is 5 A and the voltage is 1 V. However, the voltage is given as 100 V, so this option is also incorrect.

The power consumed is 500 Watts.Power is defined as the rate at which energy is consumed. It is calculated by multiplying the voltage by the current. In this case, the voltage is 100 V and the current is 5 A, so the power is:Power = Voltage * Current = 100 V * 5 A = 500 WattsThe answer is 500.
This is the power consumed if the current is 5 A and the voltage is 1000 V. However, the voltage is given as 100 V, so this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option A: This is the power consumed if the current is 0.05 A. However, the current is given as 5 A, so this option is incorrect. Option B: This is the power consumed if the current is 5 A and the voltage is 1 V. However, the voltage is given as 100 V, so this option is also incorrect. Option C: The power consumed is 500 Watts.Power is defined as the rate at which energy is consumed. It is calculated by multiplying the voltage by the current. In this case, the voltage is 100 V and the current is 5 A, so the power is:Power = Voltage * Current = 100 V * 5 A = 500 WattsThe answer is 500. Option D: This is the power consumed if the current is 5 A and the voltage is 1000 V. However, the voltage is given as 100 V, so this option is also incorrect.

questions.text: If two or more resistors are joined to each other side by side, this combination is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a series combination, the resistors are connected end to end that the current flowing through one resistor also flows through the others.

When two or more resistors are joined side by side, this combination is called "parallel." In a parallel circuit, the voltage across each resistor is the same, and the total current is the sum of the currents through each resistor.

The Y-Delta transformation, also known as the "Y-Delta conversion" or "Y-Delta equivalence," is a technique used to simplify and analyze complex resistor networks. It allows us to convert a circuit with resistors connected in a Y (or "star") configuration into an equivalent circuit with resistors connected in a Delta (or "triangle") configuration, and vice versa.In a Y configuration, three resistors are connected together in a shape resembling the letter Y. Each resistor is connected between a common point called the "node" and one of the three branches of the Y.In a Delta configuration, three resistors are connected together in a triangular shape. Each resistor is connected between two of the three nodes or connection points of the Delta.
This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: When two or more resistors are joined side by side, this combination is called "parallel." In a parallel circuit, the voltage across each resistor is the same, and the total current is the sum of the currents through each resistor.

questions.text: The reciprocal of resistivity is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistance is a property of a material that opposes the flow of electric current. It is measured in ohms (Ω) and is the ratio of voltage to current in a circuit.The reciprocal of resistance is conductance.

Inductance is a property in electrical circuits that describes the ability of a coil or wire to store energy in a magnetic field when an electric current is passed through it.The reciprocal of inductance is called "capacitance."

Conductivity is the measure of a material's ability to conduct electric current. It is the reciprocal of resistivity.The higher the conductivity, the better the material is at allowing the flow of electric current.

Flexibility is not directly related to electrical properties like resistivity or conductivity. It refers to the ability of a material to bend, deform, or flex without breaking.There is no reciprocal term specifically associated with flexibility in the context of electrical properties.

questions.explanation: Conductivity is the measure of a material's ability to conduct electric current. It is the reciprocal of resistivity.The higher the conductivity, the better the material is at allowing the flow of electric current

questions.text: Two long, parallel conductors which are free to move are arranged 1.0 cm apart. A steady current of 20 A flows in each of the conductors in the same direction. The conductors:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Remains stationary: This would only be true if there was no current or if the currents were flowing in opposite directions.
When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, they exert equal and opposite attractive forces on each other. This causes them to move towards one another.
Move away from each other: This would happen if the currents were flowing in opposite directions, creating a repulsive force.

options.text: Move at right angles to each other
options.explanation: Move at right angles to each other: The force between the conductors is either attractive or repulsive, acting along the line connecting them, not at right angles.

questions.explanation: When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, they exert equal and opposite attractive forces on each other. This causes them to move towards one another.

questions.text: An electron enters in a magnetic field at an angle less than 90 degrees with magnetic field. Which of the following path is followed by an electron?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An electron would follow a straight path if its velocity is completely parallel to the magnetic field lines (θ=0 or θ=180). In this case, there is no perpendicular component of velocity to cause circular or helical motion.

An electron will follow a circular path when it enters the magnetic field at an angle of 90 degrees to the field lines. This is because the entire velocity is perpendicular to the field, creating a centripetal force that results in circular motion.

A curved path is not a complete description. While the electron initially follows a curved trajectory when entering the field, it ultimately forms a helical path due to the combination of circular and linear motion.

When an electron enters a magnetic field at an angle between 0 and 90 degrees, its motion is helical. This is due to the presence of both perpendicular and parallel components of velocity relative to the magnetic field. The perpendicular component leads to circular motion, while the parallel component allows for linear motion, resulting in a helical path.

questions.explanation: When an electron enters a magnetic field at an angle less than 90 degrees, its velocity can be decomposed into components: one parallel and one perpendicular to the magnetic field. The perpendicular component of the velocity causes the electron to move in a circular path due to the Lorentz force, while the parallel component causes it to move linearly along the direction of the magnetic field. The combination of these two motions results in a helical path.The option 'Straight' is incorrect because it applies only when the velocity is completely parallel to the magnetic field. 'Circular' is incorrect as this occurs only when the velocity is entirely perpendicular to the field. 'Curve' is partially correct initially but does not describe the continuous motion, which is helical.

questions.text: Lenz’s law in electromagnetic induction is the direct consequence of the principle of conservation of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Lenz’s law is used to determine the direction of the induced current by a change in magnetic field. This direction of the induced current always opposes the direction of the magnetic field which produced it.Complete step by step answer:Lenz’s law states that the direction of an induced current by a changing magnetic field is such that the magnetic field created by the induced current opposes that change responsible for its production. This means, a change in magnetic field (B1) produces an induced current and the induce current also generates some magnetic field (B2) which is different from the initial magnetic field (B1). Now, the generated magnetic field (B2) by the induced current opposes the initial changing magnetic field (B1). Consider a magnet and a coil, such that the magnet is approaching towards the coil. When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards a coil, the face of the coil near to the magnet behaves like a north pole and tries to repel the approaching North Pole. To do this, the coil generates an anticlockwise current in itself. Here, we can see that electrical energy is not generated just by moving the magnet towards the coil. There is an opposite for the change in flux. Hence we can say that Lenz’s law is a consequence of conservation of energy. Thus, the right answer is Option A; Lenz’s law is a consequence of law of conservation of energy.
Lenz’s law is a consequence of law of conservation of energy.
Lenz’s law is a consequence of law of conservation of energy.
Lenz’s law is a consequence of law of conservation of energy.

questions.explanation: Lenz’s law is used to determine the direction of the induced current by a change in magnetic field. This direction of the induced current always opposes the direction of the magnetic field which produced it.Complete step by step answer:Lenz’s law states that the direction of an induced current by a changing magnetic field is such that the magnetic field created by the induced current opposes that change responsible for its production. This means, a change in magnetic field (B1) produces an induced current and the induce current also generates some magnetic field (B2) which is different from the initial magnetic field (B1). Now, the generated magnetic field (B2) by the induced current opposes the initial changing magnetic field (B1). Consider a magnet and a coil, such that the magnet is approaching towards the coil. When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards a coil, the face of the coil near to the magnet behaves like a north pole and tries to repel the approaching North Pole. To do this, the coil generates an anticlockwise current in itself. Here, we can see that electrical energy is not generated just by moving the magnet towards the coil. There is an opposite for the change in flux. Hence we can say that Lenz’s law is a consequence of conservation of energy. Thus, the right answer is Option A; Lenz’s law is a consequence of law of conservation of energy.

questions.text: If a wire having current 10A has a 3T magnetic field, what will be the magnetic field at a point from the wire at double the distance?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Any current-carrying wire is surrounded by the magnetic field which is: B = µ0I/2πr It can be seen from the above equation that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the distance and directly proportional to the current. When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.
When distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.
When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.

questions.explanation: Any current-carrying wire is surrounded by the magnetic field which is B = µ0I/2πr. It can be seen from the above equation that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the distance and directly proportional to the current. When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will reduce by a factor of two, which is 1.5 T.

questions.text: Is it possible to separate north pole only from bar magnet?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because you cannot separate only the north pole from a bar magnet. A bar magnet has two poles, north and south, and they cannot be isolated from each other.

Magnetism is a property that is caused due to separation of poles at atomic level. Even when the north pole is separated from the south pole, a new north pole is formed. So, we cannot separate the north pole from a bar magnet. Hence, option B is the correct option.

options.text: In some cases it is possible
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because in all cases, you cannot separate only the north pole from a bar magnet. The magnet's poles are an intrinsic property, and they always come together as a pair.
This option is also not correct.

questions.explanation: Magnetism is a property that is caused due to separation of poles at atomic level. Even when the north pole is separated from the south pole, a new north pole is formed. So, we cannot separate the north pole from a bar magnet. Hence, option B is the correct option.

questions.text: A force which is experienced in a magnetic field depends on:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The force depends on the magnitude of the charge, as described in the formula F = qvB sin(θ).

options.text: speed of the moving charge v
options.explanation: The force depends on the velocity of the charge, as indicated in the formula F = qvB sin(θ).
The force depends on the magnetic field strength, as given by the formula F = qvB sin(θ).

The magnetic force on a charge is determined by the product of the charge magnitude, its velocity, and the magnetic field strength, as expressed in F = qvB sin(θ). Therefore, it depends on all these factors.
This is incorrect as the force does depend on the charge, velocity, and magnetic field strength, as per the formula F = qvB sin(θ).

questions.explanation: The force experienced by a charge moving in a magnetic field is given by the formula F = qvB sin(θ), where q is the charge, v is the velocity of the charge, and B is the magnetic field strength. The angle θ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field lines. Therefore, the force depends on the magnitude of the charge, the speed of the moving charge, and the strength of the magnetic field. Option D correctly identifies all these factors. Options A, B, and C each identify only one of these factors, which is insufficient on its own. Option E is incorrect as it suggests that the force depends on none of these factors, which is contrary to the established formula.

questions.text: If a constant current is flowing through a coil, then the flux through that coil will become constant. In such types of arrangements, the electromotive force (emf) induced in the coil will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Self-SInductance :The self-induced emf is also called the back emf as it opposes any change in the current in a circuit. Physically, the self-inductance plays the role of inertia. It is the electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics
Zero :In the case given above there is a constant flow of current which means there is no change in flux so the induced E.M.F is zero because induced emf depends upon the time rate change of flux.

Mutual-Inductance :When two coils are brought in proximity with each other the magnetic field in one of the coils tend to link with the other. This further leads to the generation of voltage in the second coil. This property of a coil which affects or changes the current and voltage in a secondary coil is called mutual inductance.

Back EMF : When the armature of a d.c. motor rotates under the influence of the driving torque, the armature conductors move through the magnetic field and hence e.m.f. is induced in them as in a generator. The induced e.m.f. acts in opposite direction to the applied voltage V (Lenz's law) and in known as back or counter e.m.f.

questions.explanation: The emf induced in a circuit is directly proportional to the time rate of change of the magnetic flux through the circuit. And if there is no change in flux so the induced emf is zero.

questions.text: A conductor carrying a current 'I' has length 'L'. When it is placed in a magnetic field 'B' at 90°, it experiences a force:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct. The force on a conductor carrying current 'I' in a magnetic field 'B' at an angle of 90° is given by the formula F = BILsin(θ). Since the angle is 90°, sin(90°) = 1, making the force BIL.
This is incorrect. The formula for force does not involve squaring the magnetic field 'B'. The correct formula is F = BILsin(θ), not B²LI.
This is incorrect. The current 'I' should not be squared in the formula for the force. The correct formula is F = BILsin(θ), not BLI².
This is incorrect. The force is zero if the angle is 0° or 180°, but here the angle is 90°, which results in a maximum force.
This is incorrect. The force is finite and given by the formula F = BILsin(θ). It cannot be infinite.

questions.explanation: The formula for the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor is BILsin(θ), where theta represents the angle between the current, I, and the magnetic field, B, hence sin(90)=1 and the value of the force will be BIL (maximum).

questions.text: Two parallel straight conductors, in which current is flowing in the same direction, attract each other. The cause of it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them. This happens because:Magnetic Fields Around the Conductors:Each current-carrying conductor generates a circular magnetic field around it, as described by Ampère's right-hand rule.Magnetic Interaction:The magnetic field of one conductor exerts a force on the second conductor and vice versa.When currents are in the same direction, the magnetic fields between the wires reinforce each other in a way that causes an attractive force.Lorentz Force Explanation:The moving charges (current) in one conductor experience a force due to the magnetic field created by the other conductor.According to the Lorentz force law, this force acts inwards, pulling the conductors together.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.

questions.explanation: Correct option is 'A'.

questions.text: Magnetic force on a moving charge depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While these factors are important, the speed of the charge is also crucial in determining the magnetic force. A stationary charge will not be affected by a magnetic field.

options.text: Magnetic field and speed of the charge only
options.explanation: The charge of the particle is also essential. A neutral particle, regardless of its speed, will not experience a magnetic force.

options.text: Charge and speed of the charge only

options.text: Magnetic field, charge, and speed of the charge
options.explanation: The magnetic force on a moving charge is calculated using F = qvBsin⁡θ, which includes the charge (q), velocity (v), magnetic field (B), and the angle (θ) between the velocity and magnetic field directions. All of these factors contribute to the magnitude of the force.

questions.explanation: The magnitude of the magnetic force between them depends on how much charge is in how much velocity in each of the two objects and magnetic field.

questions.text: The SI unit of magnetic flux is Weber which is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The unit NA/m is the unit of magnetic field strength, also known as magnetic field intensity or magnetic field intensity. It represents the SI unit for magnetic field strength and is measured in amperes per meter (A/m).

The unit NmA is not a recognized unit in the SI system. Newton-meter (Nm) is the SI unit of torque or moment of force, representing the product of a force (measured in newtons) and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (measured in meters). Milliampere (mA) is the SI unit for electrical current, representing one thousandth of an ampere. Combining the two units, NmA, would not have a specific meaning or representation in a recognized SI unit.

The unit Nm^-1A^-1 represents the reciprocal of the unit for electrical resistance, which is ohm (Ω). Reciprocal of ohm: (Ω)^-1 = Ω^-1 = Nm^-1A^-1 Therefore, Nm^-1A^-1 is the unit of electrical conductance, which is the measure of how easily electric current can flow through a material. It is commonly denoted by the unit siemens (S) in the SI system.

The SI unit of magnetic flux is indeed the Weber (Wb). The Weber is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that passes through a surface of one square meter (1 m²) perpendicular to a magnetic field of one Tesla (1 T). In terms of base SI units, one Weber is equivalent to one Tesla meter squared (T·m²).

questions.explanation: The formula for the magnetic field density (B) is: B=F/IL {where F is the force, I is the current and L is the length of the conductor.} Substitute the SI units into the equation: B=N/Am The magnetic flux is BA therefore: B=N/Am x m2 =NmA-1

questions.text: The magnetic lines of force are directed in a manner that they:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Originate at south pole and terminate at north pole
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The magnetic lines of force originate at the north pole and terminate at the south pole.

This is correct. The magnetic lines of force are continuous, so they must pass through the magnet. They do not pass through the magnet in a straight line, but rather they form loops that go around the magnet.

options.text: Originate at north pole and terminate at south pole
options.explanation: The magnetic lines of force are directed in a manner that they originate at the north pole and terminate at the south pole. This is because the north pole of a magnet is a region where the magnetic field lines are pointing outwards, and the south pole of a magnet is a region where the magnetic field lines are pointing inwards.The magnetic lines of force are continuous, so they must pass through the magnet. They do not pass through the magnet in a straight line, but rather they form loops that go around the magnet.The magnetic lines of force do not go away from both the poles. If they did, then the magnetic field would be zero everywhere outside the magnet. However, the magnetic field is not zero outside the magnet. It is strongest near the poles of the magnet, and it decreases as you move away from the poles.

options.text: Go away from both the poles
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The magnetic lines of force do not go away from both the poles. If they did, then the magnetic field would be zero everywhere outside the magnet. However, the magnetic field is not zero outside the magnet. It is strongest near the poles of the magnet, and it decreases as you move away from the poles.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: The magnetic lines of force originate at the north pole and terminate at the south pole. This is because magnetic field lines emerge from the north pole and enter the south pole, completing a loop.Option A is incorrect because it reverses the direction of the magnetic lines of force, which do not originate at the south pole.Option B is incorrect in context because, while magnetic lines do pass through the magnet as continuous loops, it does not address their directional origin and termination.Option D is incorrect because magnetic lines of force do not move away from both poles; they form loops, ensuring the magnetic field is present outside the magnet.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure:By next Saturday, I _ (work) on this painting for exactly one month.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A is correct as the sentence is in future perfect continuous tense. The phrase, “By next Saturday”, indicates that the sentence is in future tense.
B is incorrect because it is applicable in the case of future continuous tense whereas the sentence is in future perfect continuous tense.
C is incorrect as it is applicable in the case of simple perfect tense whereas the sentence is in future perfect continuous tense.
D is incorrect as it is applicable in the case of past tense whereas the sentence is in future.

questions.explanation: Correct option is A because “By next Saturday” indicates future tense and option A displays perfect future tense structure.

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of subject-verb agreement:The peon will not _ the bell yet.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A is incorrect as it is applicable in case of present tense whereas the sentence is in future.
B is incorrect as it is applicable in the case of Past perfect tense whereas the sentence is in future.
C is correct as the sentence is in future perfect tense.
D is incorrect as it is applicable in case of present tense whereas the sentence is in future.

questions.explanation: Option C displays perfect past tense as the auxiliary verb “have” appears before the main verb “rung”.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: The baby _ for milk now.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) Cry: The option "cry" is in the present tense, but it does not match the subject "baby," which is singular. The correct verb form for the singular subject "baby" in the present tense would be "cries." So, this option is not correct.
b) Cried: The option "cried" is in the past tense, but the sentence is about the present time (now). As a result, this option is not correct.
c) Cries: The option "cries" is in the present tense and matches the subject "baby," which is singular.

d) Is crying: The option "is crying" is in the present continuous tense, and while it does convey the idea that the baby is currently crying for milk, it is not the most natural or straightforward way to complete the sentence.

questions.explanation: As the sentence is in the present tense ( the statement says “milk now”), the missing verb must be a continuous present verb.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: Where were you _ at that time?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Going: is correct as it is a present perfect continuous word and also makes grammatical sense when said out loud.
Goes: is incorrect as the sentence does not make grammatical sense when said out loud.
Went: is incorrect as the sentence does not make grammatical sense when said out loud
Gone: is incorrect as it is in past tense and the sentence is grammatically incorrect.

The definite article "the" is used before singular or plural nouns when we are referring to something specific that is known to the speaker and listener or has been previously mentioned or is identifiable from the context.
This option is incorrect as "a" will be used.

questions.text: Choose the correct sentence.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: After “did not,” the main verb must be in its base form, not past tense. So it should be “accompany”, not “accompanied.”
After “do not”, the main verb must be in its base form, not past tense. So it should be “accompany”, not “accompanied.”

The subject “I” correctly pairs with the base form of the verb “accompany” using do not for negation.The sentence is in the present simple tense, correctly expressing a habitual or general action, which is appropriate here.

questions.explanation: The subject “I” correctly pairs with the base form of the verb “accompany” using do not for negation.The sentence is in the present simple tense, correctly expressing a habitual or general action, which is appropriate here.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: It was so quiet you _ (can) hear a pin drop.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) Can: The option "can" is in the present tense, but the sentence is describing a past situation ("It was so quiet"). The correct form for the past tense of "can" is "could." So, this option is not correct.

b) Could: The option "could" is in the past tense and matches the description of the situation being in the past ("It was so quiet"). "Could" is the correct past tense form of "can," and it indicates the ability to hear a pin drop in the quiet environment. This option correctly completes the sentence.

c) Shall: The option "shall" is not the appropriate word to complete the sentence. "Shall" is a modal verb used to express future possibility or intention, but it does not fit in the context of the sentence describing a past situation.

d) Will: The option "will" is also not the correct choice for completing the sentence. "Will" is a modal verb used to express future actions or certainty, which does not fit the context of describing a past quiet moment.

questions.explanation: As the sentence is in the past tense (the word “was so quiet” was used), the missing word has to be in the past tense. Hence ‘could’ is most suitable as it is also grammatically correct when said out loud.

As the word ‘being' is used, the sentence is in the present tense hence the verbs "was" is incorrect.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: My friends and I enjoy doing many of the same things. In that respect, we have a lot _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is not the correct choice. The phrase "in similar" is not idiomatic and does not fit the context of the sentence. It does not convey the intended meaning.

This option is not the correct choice either. The phrase "in particular" means specifically or especially, but it does not fit in the context of expressing shared interests between the speaker and their friends.

This option is the correct choice. The phrase "in common" means having shared interests, experiences, or characteristics. It is the most appropriate phrase to complete the sentence and indicates that the speaker and their friends share many things they enjoy doing together.
This option is not the correct choice. The phrase "in comparison" is used to compare two or more things, which is not what the sentence is trying to convey.

questions.explanation: As the individuals enjoy many of the same, familiar things, they enjoy doing many ‘common’ things together and hence have a lot in common.

questions.text: Look at this series 7, 10, 8, 11, 9, 12. Which number do you think will be next?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: By examining the given series 7, 10, 8, 11, 9, and 12, we can identify a pattern where numbers alternate between increasing by 3 and decreasing by 2.To illustrate, we start with 7 and add 3 to obtain 10, then subtract 2 to yield 8. Continuing this pattern, we add 3 to 8 to get 11, subtract 2 to arrive at 9, and so forth.Applying this pattern to the series, the next number can be determined by subtracting 2 from 12, we get 10.

questions.text: Statement:The availability of imported fruits has increased in the indigenous market and so the demand for indigenous fruits has been decreased.Courses of Action:I. To help the indigenous producers of fruits, the Government should impose a high import duty on these fruits, even if they are not of good quality.II. The fruit vendors should stop selling imported fruits. So that the demand for indigenous fruits would increase.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Both the courses of actions follow
options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.The ideas suggested in both courses of action I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better-quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.

options.text: None of the courses of actions follow
options.explanation: The ideas suggested in both courses of action I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. Hence, neither I nor II follow.

The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better-quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.

options.text: Only course of action II follows

The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better-quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.

The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.
